summary:
Woodblock printing (developed in China c. 7th–9th centuries AD) was the first practical printing technology — the reproduction of text and images by carving a design in reverse into a wooden block, inking it, and pressing paper against it — and represents one of the most consequential inventions in human history. The technology was initially applied to Buddhist texts and images (the oldest dated printed book using this method is the [[event:2159|Diamond Sutra]], 868 AD), then rapidly expanded to secular texts, government documents, and eventually commercial publishing. The Chinese government used woodblock printing to produce the world's first paper money ([[event:2217|Chinese paper money]]) and official gazettes.

Woodblock printing transformed Chinese civilization: the cost of producing books fell dramatically, enabling wider literacy and the standardization of classical texts; the examination system that staffed the Chinese bureaucracy was facilitated by printed study materials; Buddhist sutras could be reproduced in editions of thousands rather than hand-copied one at a time; and almanacs, agricultural manuals, and medical texts reached audiences that hand-copying could never have served. Movable type (individual ceramic characters) was invented in China by Bi Sheng around 1040 AD during the [[event:2156|Song dynasty]], but the enormous number of Chinese characters (tens of thousands) made movable type less practical than in alphabetic writing systems. The technology spread from China to Korea and Japan, and via Central Asia to the Islamic world; European movable type printing (Gutenberg, c. 1450) was likely independently invented but may have been influenced by Asian printing technology transmitted through trade.
